coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor for triticonazole based on the encapsulated boron-doped and phosphorous-doped carbon dots in the metal organic framework
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shokri, Roghayeh
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In this work, boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) with blue fluorescence and phosphorous-doped green emitting CDs (P-CDs) were encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to prepare a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor for triticonazole. The B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite exhibited two emission peaks at 440 nm and 510 nm under a single wavelength excitation of 385 nm that respectively belong to B-CDs and P-CDs. In the presence of triticonazole, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remarkably declined while that of P-CDs remained unchanged. With increasing concentration of triticonazole, the fluorescence color of the ratiometric probe progressively changed from blue to green. Under the optimized conditions, B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 probe showed a high sensitivity with a linear range from 10 to 400 nM and a detection limit of 4.0 nM for triticonazole. The probe not only has an improved sensitivity through the accumulation of analyte molecules into the metal-organic framework but also has the advantages of ratiometric fluorescence measurements in terms of precision and accuracy. The applicability of the sensor was evaluated in the analysis of water and fruit juice samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Metal–organic frameworks
Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8
Fluorescence sensor
Triticonazole

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of triticonazole by using B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs, P-CDs, ZIF-8, B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8. Inset: photographs of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 under the visible light (left) and UV lamp at 360 nm (right).
XRD patterns of ZIF-8, mixture of B-QDs and P-CDs and B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8.
a) TEM image and b) SEM image of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8, c) BET isotherms of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8.

a) Fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 in the presence of different amounts of triticonazole at optimum conditions, b) color changes of the dual emission B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 upon the addition of Triticonazole under a UV lamp, c) calibration curve for triticonazole in the range from 10 to 400 nM.
Fluorescence response of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 for 50 nM of some triazoles.
Effect of interfering species on the determination of triticonazole (100 nM).

row.entry: Tolerance limit (interferent to analyte concentration ratio)
Ag+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, I−, Br−, SO4 2−

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of triticonazole in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor for triticonazole based on the encapsulated boron-doped and phosphorous-doped carbon dots in the metal organic framework

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) with blue fluorescence and phosphorous-doped green emitting CDs (P-CDs) were encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to prepare a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor for triticonazole. The B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite exhibited two emission peaks at 440 nm and 510 nm under a single wavelength excitation of 385 nm that respectively belong to B-CDs and P-CDs. In the presence of triticonazole, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs remarkably declined while that of P-CDs remained unchanged. With increasing concentration of triticonazole, the fluorescence color of the ratiometric probe progressively changed from blue to green. Under the optimized conditions, B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 probe showed a high sensitivity with a linear range from 10 to 400 nM and a detection limit of 4.0 nM for triticonazole. The probe not only has an improved sensitivity through the accumulation of analyte molecules into the metal-organic framework but also has the advantages of ratiometric fluorescence measurements in terms of precision and accuracy. The applicability of the sensor was evaluated in the analysis of water and fruit juice samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which possess several well-known properties such as strong, stable and tunable photoluminescence, nontoxicity, biocompatibility and good water-solubility, have been widely considered as the excellent fluorescence probes [1,2]. Doping CDs with various elements modifies their optical and electronic properties and gives them some unique characters for particular applications. Recently CDs have been doped with boron and phosphorous to generate p-type CDs with excess holes and n-type CDs with excess electrons, respectively. This is lead to a change in the physicochemical properties of CDs [3–5]. As a result, the photoluminescence behavior as well as the selectivity and sensitivity of boron-doped CDs (B-CDs) or phosphorous-doped CDs (P-CDs) toward particular analytes may be different from conventional CDs. This potentially broadens the applicability of CDs for sensing of various types of molecules [6–11]. CDs with different photoluminescence behaviors can also be applied for developing ratiometric fluorescence assays, in which the ratio of fluorescence intensity at two different emission wavelengths under one excitation wavelength is measured. Unlike conventional single-wavelength methods, ratiometric probes are not prone to problems such as fluctuation of the excitation light intensity, instrumental efficiency and probe concentration. Therefore, the signal-to-noise ratio, sensitivity and accuracy of such probes are improved [12,13].

A class of crystalline microporous materials is metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) which formed by metal ions or clusters linked together by poly-functional organic bridging ligands [14]. Due to having some distinguished properties such as high porosity and wide surface area, uniform shapes, tunable pore sizes and large diversity in structure, MOFs have been promising for many applications including gas and small molecules storage and separation [15,16], catalysis [17], biomedical [18], drug delivery [19] and sensing [20]. The strong encapsulation ability of MOFs allows guest molecules or nanoparticles with proper characteristics to be embedded in MOFs as the host. This opportunity can be utilized to combine the properties of the guest with those of MOF host in order to enhance their behaviors [21]. ZIF-8 is one of the classical MOFs which are constructed of Zn ions and 2-methylimidazole linkers. The characteristics of ZIF-8 such as simple synthesis, water solubility, stable structure and high loading capacity, make it a good candidate for various applications [22,23]. Lin et al. [24] reported the encapsulation of fluorescent CDs in ZIF-8 for enhancing the fluorescence sensing ability. MOFs have also been applied for developing ratiometric fluorescent probes. Some of these probes have been designed based on MOFs which have intrinsic fluorescence [25–27], while there are some other probes based on the encapsulation of two different fluorescent nanomaterials in MOFs to achievement ratiometric fluorescence sensors [22,28,29]. To the best of our knowledge, ratiometric fluorescence assays based on encapsulation of B-CDs and P-CDs in MOFs have not previously been reported.

Triazole fungicides are widely used as combatant for plant pathogens and plant growth regulating in agriculture [30]. However, owing to their high toxicity and long persistence, these fungicides have harmful effects on environment and human health [31]. A broad-spectrum systemic triazole fungicide with widespread usage as a seed treatment against foliar diseases is triticonazole (Fig. S1 (ESM)) [32–34]. Same as other triazole fungicides, triticonazole residues cause health hazards and environmental pollutions, so developing simple, fast and sensitive methods for its monitoring in real environmental samples is of great importance.

In this study, a dual-emissive MOF ratiometric probe is reported for determination of triticonazole through encapsulating blue-emitting B-CDs and green emitting P-CDs into ZIF-8 nanostructure. In the presence of triticonazole the fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs decreased while the intensity of P-CDs remained unchanged. The highly porous structure of MOF leads to the accumulation of analyte and amplification of the sensitivity of the probe.

A JASCO FP-8300 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan, www.jascoinc.com) with a 1.0 cm quartz cell was used to record the fluorescence spectra and a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia), to record the UV–vis absorption spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany, www.zeiss.com) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Mira 3 FEG, Tescan Co., Czech Republic, www.tescan.com) were used to characterize the size and morphology of BCDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8. A Quantachrom C Instrument (Chem BET-3000, Quantachrom C., USA) was used to specify Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area by nitrogen adsorption/desorption at 77 K. A Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Co., Germany, www.bruker.com) was used to perform FT-IR measurement. Siemens D5000 instrument (Germany) was used to obtain X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns.

All reagents were of analytical-reagent grade. Deionized water (obtained from aqua MAX - Ultra 370 water purification system, Young Lin instrument Co., Korea) was used throughout the experiment. Boric acid, Trisodium citrate, Glucose, Zinc Nitrate, 2-methylimidazole were procured from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany, www.merckmillipore.com). Triticonazole was provided by GYAH Co. (Karaj, Iran). A stock solution of triticonazole with concentration of 100 μM was prepared in a minimum volume of methanol and diluting with water and stored at 4 °C. Working solutions were prepared daily by proper dilution of the stock solution with deionized water.

B-CDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using trisodium citrate and boric acid. First, 0.5 g trisodium citrate and 0.1 g boric acid were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water. The mixture was then taken to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 18 h. The resulting yellow solution displayed blue fluorescence under UV light.

P-CDs were prepared according to a published method [35]. First, 0.7 g glucose was dissolved in 2 mL deionized water and 5 mL concentrated phosphoric acid. Then the homogeneous solution was heated in the oven for 20 min at 90 °C. The pH of solution was adjusted to 7 with NaOH solution (1 M). Afterwards, light brown solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min to separate large particles. The synthesized P-CDs displayed green fluorescence under UV light.

The B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite was prepared through a procedure reported in the literatures with minor modification [36,37]. Briefly, 0.65 g 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in 20 mL methanol and 0.3 g Zn(NO3)2.6H2O was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water. Under magnetic stirring, the zinc nitrate solution was rapidly poured into 2-methylimidazole methanolic solution. Then without delay, 1.0 mL of B-CDs and 1.0 mL of P-CDs solutions were added into the mixture. After 1 h stirring, B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 was resulted as a white precipitate. The composite was gathered by centrifugation and washed several times with methanol. Finally, B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite was re-dispersed in 10 mL deionized water for further uses.

Typically, 1.0 mL of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 suspension (1.2 mg/mL) and 200 μL of 0.12 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0) were added to a centrifuge tube. Then, a proper amount of triticonazole standard or sample solution was added and final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 3.0 mL with deionized water. After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 440 and 510 nm upon an excitation wavelength at 385 nm.

The apple juice sample was filtrated by a filter paper (ALBET, DP 135 125, Barcelona, Spain) and diluted 5-fold. The water samples were utilized without any pretreatment. For recovery experiments, the samples were spiked with proper amounts of triticonazole standard solution before any pretreatment and analyzed according to the general procedure.

For construction of MOF-based composite sensor, we first synthesized a blue-emitting B-CD and a green-emitting P-CD by using simple methods. Then these CDs were simultaneously embedded in ZIF-8 nanostructure. Fig. 1 displays fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs, P-CDs, pure ZIF-8 and B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite. B-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence emission at 440 nm with an excitation of 385 nm. Under the same excitation wavelength, P-CDs exhibit emission peak at 510 nm. Pure ZIF-8 exhibits no obvious fluorescence, but B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite demonstrates dual emission at 440 and 510 nm upon a single excitation wavelength at 385 nm, same as mixture of B-CDs and P-CDs. The B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 composite shows a bright blue fluorescence under the UV light (inset of Fig. 1).

The XRD patterns of ZIF-8, mixture of B-CDs and P-CDs, and B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 2 ) indicate that the crystal structure of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8 are alike. The peak at 2θ = 7.3° which attributed to a 110 peak proves that the MOF has a rhombic dodecahedron-shaped crystal [38]. Similarity of XRD pattern of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 with that of pure ZIF-8 and the fact that the characteristic peak of B-CD/P-CD mixture does not exist in the XRD pattern of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 demonstrate that the incorporation of B-CDs and P-CDs has not changed the crystal structure of ZIF-8.

TEM and SEM images of the composite (Fig. 3 ) confirm the rhombic dodecahedron structure of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 and reveal that the nanoparticles are monodisperse and their mean size are approximately 75 nm. TEM image of B-CDs is also presented in Fig. S2, indicating a spherical shape and the average diameter of 4 ± 2 nm.

As shown in Fig. S3 the FTIR spectrum of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 is combination of FTIR spectra of ZIF-8 and B-QD/P-CD mixture. For ZIF-8, the peaks at 1330 and 1110 cm−1 correspond to CN bonds in imidazole group and the peak at 682 cm−1 corresponds to ZnN bond. These peaks were also found in B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 spectrum [39]. The mixture of B-QDs and P-CDs exhibits the absorption band of -OH around 3400 cm−1 which is also observed in the spectrum of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) isotherm of ZIF-8 and B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 in Fig. 3c, demonstrate a typical type I isotherm with 1486 and 1135 m2g−1of BET surface area respectively. These results are consistent with the previous reports [40]. The pore size distribution (PSD) of ZIF-8 and BC Ds/P-CDs@ZIF-8 were obtained by nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT) using a demo version of SAIEUS software (www.nldft.com/download/) (Fig. S4). The results reveal micropores with an average pore size of 1.2 nm for both of them. Since cavities are too small for incorporation of nanoparticles inside them, therefore, the PSD of ZIF-8 and BC Ds/P-CDs@ZIF-8 are same. On the other hand the surface area of composite is lower than ZIF-8, so it is probable that CDs are adsorbed on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of triticonazole by using B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that triticonazole remarkably reduced the fluorescence of B-CDs but it had no effect on the P-CDs. Therefore, we can use ratiometric method for analysis. In this method we need two wavelengths with different behavior toward analyte, which provide two signals: response signal and reference signal. Fluorescence intensity of B-CDs (at 440 nm) was taken as the response signal while that of P-CDs (at 510 nm) was used as a reference signal for ratiometric measurements. It should be mentioned that the influence of this fungicide was also tested on some other kinds of CDs, containing citric acid-derived CDs (with fluorescence maximum of 470 nm) and S,N-CDs (with fluorescence maximum of 425 nm) but no significant effect was observed. Based on this unique behavior of B-CDs, a ratiometric probe can be constructed for the determination of triticonazole. Moreover, in order to achieve high sensitivity, the possibility of using MOF was considered. As Fig. S5 indicates, the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 in the presence of triticonazole (200 nM) is much larger than the decrease in the fluorescence of B-CD/P-CD mixture with the same amount of triticonazole. This higher sensitivity is originated from the high porosity of ZIF-8 structure and so its high sorption ability that resulted to the accumulation of the analyte.

In order to obtain the highest response for triticonazole, the effect of some assay conditions including pH of the solution, amount of the composite and incubation time on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was investigated. The analytical response used for this purpose was quenching ratio defined as F440/F510. The obtained results from these experiments are shown in Fig. S6 (ESM). The highest fluorescence quenching was obtained at pH 6.0 with phosphate buffer, 1.0 mL of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 solution and an incubation time of 5.0 min. Fig. S7 (ESM) shows that phosphate buffer does not have considerable effect on the fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8.

The analytical performance of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 for determination of triticonazole, under the optimized conditions was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of the probe in the presence of different concentration of triticonazole are displayed in Fig. 4a. As can be seen, upon increasing the concentration of triticonazole, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 at 440 nm decreased continuously while the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm remained constant. The fluorescence color of the solutions changed continuously from blue to green upon addition of triticonazole which can be clearly observed form Fig. 4b.

A good linear relationship (R = 0.997) between the quenching efficiency [Δ(F440/F510): (F440/F510)0–(F440/F510), where (F440/F510)0 and (F440/F510) are the ratio of fluorescent intensity at 440 nm to 510 nm in the absence and presence of triticonazole, respectively] and concentration of triticonazole is obtained in the range of 10–400 nM (Fig. 4c). The detection limit (LOD) for triticonazole was calculated to be 4.0 nM and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for five repeated measurements of 100 nM triticonazole was achieved to be 2.4%.

For comparison, the ratiometric sensing of triticonazole by using B-QD/P-CD mixture was also examined. We found that the linear range of mixture was narrower and sensitivity of that was lower (80–300 nM with a detection limit of 32 nM) than B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 probe.

Comparison of the probe with other reported methods for triticonazole demonstrates that the detection limit of our method is better than or comparable with that of supercritical fluid chromatography (FC) (~2000 nM) [33], Liquid chromatography–MS/MS method (~80 nM) [41] and HPLC (~5 nM) [34]. Moreover compared to chromatographic assays, our method is simple and quick and does not need complex pre-separation procedures and expensive and sophisticated instrumentation.

The possible mechanisms for the fluorescence decline were examined. The energy and electron transfer from B-CD to triticonazole is not possible because there is no overlap between the emission spectrum of the B-CDs and absorption band of triticonazole (Fig. S8a, ESM) and the absorption band of triticonazole has lower energy than the band gap of B-CDs (Fig. S8b, ESM). Also electron transfer from triticonazole to B-CDs cannot occur because at the chosen excitation wavelength (385 nm) triticonazole molecules with absorption peak at 260 nm cannot be excited.

Considering that B-CDs with extra holes can act as a hole donor [3] and amine groups can act as hole acceptors [42,43], the probable mechanism can be hole transfer from B-CDs to triticonazole molecules which results in the reduction of luminescent centers of B-CDs [44]. The fact that triticonazole has no effect of P-CDs or un-doped CDs, supports this mechanism.

The response of the B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 probe for 50 nM of some triazole pesticides (tebuconazole, diniconazole, penconazole) under the optimized conditions are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen triticonazole exhibited the highest sensitivity. Moreover, the effect of some common inorganic ions on the determination of 100 nM triticonazole was examined. The tolerance limits (i.e. the concentration ratio of interfering species to analyte that produces relative error of 5% or less) are given in Table 1 . These results show that B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probe possesses a high selectivity for the determination triticonazole in real samples.

The method was applied to the determination of triticonazole in real water and juice samples. Recovery studies were accomplished by spiking the samples with various concentration of triticonazole. The estimated mean concentration for each sample was summarized in Table 2 . Statistical analysis of these results using Student's t-test demonstrated that there are no significant differences between added and found values. These results confirm that B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 can be applied for analysis of real samples.

It was shown that B-CDs as the p-type carbon nanomaterials uniquely respond to triticonazole while P-CDs (and also other types of CDs) do not. Based on this fact, a rapid, highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence probe was designed for the determination of triticonazole. B-CDs and P-CDs which both of them were synthesized via simple, low-cost and environment-friendly methods were embedded within ZIF-8 as a host MOF. The high sensitivity of B-CDs/P-CDs@ZIF-8 was originated from its capability to accumulate the analyte due to having great surface area and high porosity. It was applied to the analysis of spiked real water and juice samples with satisfactory recoveries.
Roghayeh Shokri: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118951.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: a review on mechanisms and applications
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Zu, F. Yan, Z. Bai, J. Xu, Y. Wang, Y. Huang, X. Zhou, The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: A review on mechanisms and applications, Microchim. Acta. 184 (2017) 1899–1914. doi:10.1007/s00604-017-2318-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots: rational synthesis, tunable optical properties and analytical applications
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, Y. Zhu, S. Yu, C. Jiang, Fluorescent carbon dots: rational synthesis, tunable optical properties and analytical applications, RSC Adv. 7 (2017) 40973–40989. doi:10.1039/C7RA07573A.

J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Barman, B. Jana, S. Bhattacharyya, A. Patra, Photophysical Properties of Doped Carbon Dots (N, P, and B) and Their Influence on Electron/Hole Transfer in Carbon Dots–Nickel (II) Phthalocyanine Conjugates, J. Phys. Chem. C. 118 (2014) 20034–20041. doi:10.1021/jp507080c.

Carbon.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.B. Bourlinos, G. Trivizas, M.A. Karakassides, M. Baikousi, A. Kouloumpis, D. Gournis, A. Bakandritsos, K. Hola, O. Kozak, R. Zboril, I. Papagiannouli, P. Carbon.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhi, M.J. Gallagher, B.P. Frank, T.Y. Lyons, T.A. Qiu, J. Da, A.C. Mensch, R.J. Hamers, Z. Rosenzweig, D.H. Fairbrother, C.L. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boron doped carbon dots with unusually high photoluminescence quantum yield for ratiometric intracellular pH sensing
ChemPhysChem.

Microchim. Acta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence quenchometric method for determination of ferric ion using boron-doped carbon dots
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Wang, Q. Hao, Y. Zhang, Y. Xu, W. Lei, Fluorescence quenchometric method for determination of ferric ion using boron-doped carbon dots, Microchim. Acta. 183 (2016) 273–279. doi:10.1007/s00604-015-1650-1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Boron doped carbon dots as a multifunctional fluorescent probe for sorbate and vitamin B12
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Jia, Y. Hu, Y. Li, Q. Zeng, X. Jiang, Z. Cheng, Boron doped carbon dots as a multifunctional fluorescent probe for sorbate and vitamin B12, Microchim. Acta. 186 (2019) 84. doi:10.1007/s00604-018-3196-5.

J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem.

Optik.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probes for chemo/biosensing and bioimaging of biomarkers
Coord. Chem. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica embedded with carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for diniconazole
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Amjadi, R. Jalili, Molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica embedded with carbon dots and semiconductor quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for diniconazole, Biosens. Bioelectron. 96 (2017) 121–126. doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.04.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal–organic framework composites: from fundamentals to applications
